findings.title: `deposit()` credits stake from the requested amount without verifying tokens were actually received

best_agent_match.title: Reentrancy across `withdraw()` and `deposit()` lets a callback token drain the same pool repeatedly

findings.title: `withdraw()` can burn user balances without paying tokens when `transfer()` fails softly

best_agent_match.title: Reentrancy across `withdraw()` and `deposit()` lets a callback token drain the same pool repeatedly

rejected_candidates.title: Reentrancy across `withdraw()` and `deposit()` lets a callback token drain the same pool repeatedly
rejected_candidates.reason: Not a standalone issue here. `withdraw()` already deducts balance before the external call, so there is no same-balance double-withdraw against honest tokens. The proposed loop still relies on the same malicious/soft-failing token behavior already captured by F-001, making it derivative rather than incremental protocol risk.

rejected_candidates.title: Anyone can permissionlessly create staking markets for arbitrary attacker-controlled tokens
rejected_candidates.reason: Permissionless token/epoch initialization alone is not sufficient harm. Any loss path still depends on the unsafe token-transfer assumptions already covered by F-001/F-003, and no cross-pool or unavoidable harm beyond the attacker-chosen token market is shown.
